questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int x^5\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $5x^4$ is the derivative of $x^5$, not its antiderivative. Integration increases the exponent by 1 and divides by the new exponent.
The Power Rule for integration states $\int x^n\,dx = \dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$. With $n=5$: $\dfrac{x^6}{6}+C$.
The Power Rule requires dividing by the new exponent: $\dfrac{x^6}{6}+C$, not $x^6+C$.
This would be the antiderivative of $x^3$. For $x^5$, add 1 to the exponent to get $x^6$, then divide by 6.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-5.A: Determine antiderivatives of functions using basic integration rules.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \cos x\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $-\sin x$ is the antiderivative of $-\cos x$, not $\cos x$. Recall $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\sin x]=\cos x$, so $\int\cos x\,dx=\sin x+C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[\sin x] = \cos x$, so $\int \cos x\,dx = \sin x + C$ by the definition of antiderivative.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[-\cos x]=\sin x$, not $\cos x$. The antiderivative of $\cos x$ is $\sin x+C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[\tan x]=\sec^2 x$, not $\cos x$. The antiderivative of $\cos x$ is $\sin x+C$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-5.A: Determine antiderivatives of functions using basic integration rules.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx$ for $x > 0$?

choices.explanation: This equals the antiderivative of $\dfrac{2}{x^3}$, not $\dfrac{1}{x}$. The correct antiderivative is $\ln x + C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[\log_{10} x]=\dfrac{1}{x\ln 10}\neq \dfrac{1}{x}$. The natural logarithm $\ln x$ (base $e$) is the correct antiderivative of $\dfrac{1}{x}$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[\ln x] = \dfrac{1}{x}$ for $x > 0$, so $\int \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx = \ln x + C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{x^2}\right] = -\dfrac{2}{x^3}\neq \dfrac{1}{x}$. The Power Rule fails at $n=-1$; the correct antiderivative is $\ln x+C$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-5.A: Determine antiderivatives of functions using basic integration rules.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: The table shows selected values of a continuous function $f$. Using a left Riemann sum with three equal subintervals of width 2, which of the following best approximates $\int_0^6 f(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Left Riemann sum uses left endpoints: $f(0)+f(2)+f(4)=3+7+11=21$. Multiply by subinterval width 2: $2\cdot 21=42$.
$66$ is the right Riemann sum: $2(f(2)+f(4)+f(6))=2(7+11+15)=2(33)=66$. The left sum uses $f(0),f(2),f(4)$, the left endpoints of each subinterval.
$54$ would be the midpoint Riemann sum if midpoint values were 9 each. The left sum uses $f(0)+f(2)+f(4)=21$, multiplied by 2 to give 42.
$36$ does not correspond to any standard Riemann sum here. The left sum uses $f(0)+f(2)+f(4)=3+7+11=21$, and multiplies by $\Delta x=2$ to get 42.

questions.unit_objective: LIM-5.A: Approximate the value of a definite integral using Riemann sums.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following best describes what the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 1, states about $F(x) = \int_a^x f(t)\,dt$?

choices.explanation: FTC Part 1 states that if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, then $F(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ is differentiable and $F'(x)=f(x)$.
$f(a)$ is a constant; $F'(x)$ is not generally constant. FTC Part 1 gives $F'(x)=f(x)$, not $f(a)$.
$F(b)-F(a)$ is the evaluation in FTC Part 2 for $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$, not the derivative of an accumulation function.
This confuses integration and differentiation. FTC Part 1 gives $F'(x)=f(x)$ directly; you do not integrate $f'(t)$ to find $F'(x)$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-5.C: Calculate derivatives of functions defined using accumulation (FTC Part 1).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: If $F(x) = \int_1^x (3t^2 + 2)\,dt$, which of the following gives $F'(x)$?

choices.explanation: $t$ is a dummy variable of integration and does not appear in $F'(x)$. FTC Part 1 gives $F'(x)=3x^2+2$.
$6x$ is the derivative of $3x^2+2$. FTC Part 1 requires only evaluating the integrand at $x$: $F'(x)=3x^2+2$.
$x^3+2x-3$ is $F(x)$ itself (antiderivative evaluated from 1 to $x$), not $F'(x)$. FTC Part 1 gives $F'(x)=3x^2+2$.
By FTC Part 1, $F'(x) = 3x^2 + 2$. The integrand evaluated at the upper limit $x$ gives the derivative directly.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-5.C: Calculate derivatives of accumulation functions using FTC Part 1.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^3 (2x+1)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: The correct antiderivative is $x^2+x$, evaluated from 0 to 3: $(9+3)-0=12$, not 6.
$9$ comes from integrating only $2x$: $\left[x^2\right]_0^3=9$. The constant term 1 contributes $\int_0^3 1\,dx=3$, giving a total of 12.
$7$ is the integrand evaluated at $x=3$: $2(3)+1=7$. This is the value of $f(3)$, not the definite integral.
Antiderivative is $x^2+x$. Evaluate: $\bigl[x^2+x\bigr]_0^3=(9+3)-(0+0)=12$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.A: Evaluate definite integrals using FTC Part 2.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int x\sin(x^2)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: This is missing the factor of $\dfrac{1}{2}$ from $du=2x\,dx$. After substituting $u=x^2$, the integral becomes $\dfrac{1}{2}\int\sin u\,du$, giving $-\dfrac{1}{2}\cos(x^2)+C$.
Let $u=x^2$, so $du=2x\,dx$, i.e., $x\,dx=\dfrac{du}{2}$. Then $\int x\sin(x^2)\,dx=\dfrac{1}{2}\int\sin u\,du=-\dfrac{1}{2}\cos u+C=-\dfrac{1}{2}\cos(x^2)+C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[x\cos(x^2)]=\cos(x^2)-2x^2\sin(x^2)\neq x\sin(x^2)$. Use $u$-substitution with $u=x^2$.
The sign is wrong. $\int\sin u\,du=-\cos u+C$, so the result is $-\dfrac{1}{2}\cos(x^2)+C$, not $+\dfrac{1}{2}\cos(x^2)+C$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.D: Evaluate integrals using $u$-substitution.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int x e^x\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[xe^x]=e^x+xe^x\neq xe^x$. Integration by parts yields $xe^x-e^x+C$.
Integration by parts: let $u=x$, $dv=e^x\,dx$, so $du=dx$, $v=e^x$. Then $\int xe^x\,dx = xe^x - \int e^x\,dx = xe^x - e^x + C$.
Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[xe^x+e^x]=2e^x+xe^x\neq xe^x$. The correct answer is $xe^x-e^x+C$.
This incorrectly treats $e^x$ as if it were a constant. Integration by parts is required: set $u=x$, $dv=e^x\,dx$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.E: Evaluate integrals using integration by parts (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \ln x\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[x\ln x]=\ln x+1\neq\ln x$. Integration by parts gives $x\ln x-x+C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[\ln(x^2)]=\dfrac{2}{x}\neq\ln x$. The antiderivative of $\ln x$ requires integration by parts and equals $x\ln x-x+C$.
$\dfrac{1}{x}$ is the derivative of $\ln x$, not its antiderivative. Use integration by parts to find $\int\ln x\,dx=x\ln x-x+C$.
Integration by parts with $u=\ln x$, $dv=dx$: $du=\dfrac{1}{x}dx$, $v=x$. So $\int\ln x\,dx=x\ln x-\int x\cdot\dfrac{1}{x}\,dx=x\ln x-x+C$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.E: Evaluate integrals using integration by parts (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: The table above shows selected values of a continuous function $f$. Which of the following gives a midpoint Riemann sum approximation of $\int_0^4 f(x)\,dx$ using 2 subintervals of equal width?

choices.explanation: Two subintervals of width 2: $[0,2]$ and $[2,4]$. Midpoints are $x=1$ and $x=3$. Midpoint sum: $\Delta x\bigl[f(1)+f(3)\bigr]=2(5+8)=2(13)=26$.
$16=2(f(0)+f(4))=2(2+6)$. These are endpoint values, not midpoints. The midpoint sum uses $f(1)$ and $f(3)$: $2(5+8)=26$.
$20=2(f(2)+f(2))=2(4+6)=20$. This does not correctly identify the midpoints. The midpoint Riemann sum uses $f(1)=5$ and $f(3)=8$: $2(13)=26$.
$22$ corresponds to using $f(0)$ and $f(2)$: $2(2+4)=12$, or other incorrect groupings. The midpoints of $[0,2]$ and $[2,4]$ are $x=1$ and $x=3$.

questions.unit_objective: LIM-5.A: Approximate definite integrals using midpoint Riemann sums.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\dfrac{d}{dx}\int_x^{x^2} \sin(t^2)\,dt$?

choices.explanation: Split using a constant $a$: $\int_x^{x^2}\sin(t^2)\,dt=-\int_a^x\sin(t^2)\,dt+\int_a^{x^2}\sin(t^2)\,dt$. Differentiating: $-\sin(x^2)\cdot 1+\sin((x^2)^2)\cdot 2x=2x\sin(x^4)-\sin(x^2)$.
The sign from the lower limit $x$ is negative, not positive. When the lower limit is a function of $x$, its contribution to the derivative is subtracted: $-\sin(x^2)$.
This omits the Chain Rule factor $\dfrac{d}{dx}[x^2]=2x$ at the upper limit. The correct derivative is $2x\sin(x^4)-\sin(x^2)$.
This neglects the contribution from the lower limit $x$. When both limits depend on $x$, each limit contributes: upper limit gives $+2x\sin(x^4)$ and lower limit gives $-\sin(x^2)$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-5.C: Differentiate accumulation functions with variable limits using FTC Part 1 and Chain Rule.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{1}{x^2 - 1}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: The signs are reversed. Partial fractions gives $A=\tfrac{1}{2}$ (for $x-1$) and $B=-\tfrac{1}{2}$ (for $x+1$), yielding $\tfrac{1}{2}\ln|x-1|-\tfrac{1}{2}\ln|x+1|+C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{2}\ln|x^2-1|\right]=\dfrac{x}{x^2-1}\neq\dfrac{1}{x^2-1}$. Partial fractions gives $\tfrac{1}{2}\ln|x-1|-\tfrac{1}{2}\ln|x+1|+C$.
Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\ln|x^2-1|]=\dfrac{2x}{x^2-1}\neq\dfrac{1}{x^2-1}$. Partial fraction decomposition is required.

Partial fractions: $\dfrac{1}{(x-1)(x+1)}=\dfrac{A}{x-1}+\dfrac{B}{x+1}$. At $x=1$: $1=2A$, so $A=\tfrac{1}{2}$. At $x=-1$: $1=-2B$, so $B=-\tfrac{1}{2}$. Integral $=\tfrac{1}{2}\ln|x-1|-\tfrac{1}{2}\ln|x+1|+C$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.F: Evaluate integrals using partial fraction decomposition (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_1^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{x^2}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\int_1^{\infty}\dfrac{1}{x^2}\,dx=\lim_{b\to\infty}\left[-\dfrac{1}{x}\right]_1^b=\lim_{b\to\infty}\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{b}+1\right)=0+1=1$.
The integral converges because $p=2>1$ in $\int_1^\infty x^{-p}\,dx$, which converges for $p>1$. The value is $\dfrac{1}{p-1}=\dfrac{1}{1}=1$.
$-1$ comes from evaluating $-\dfrac{1}{x}$ at $x=1$ without applying the limit at $\infty$. Properly, $\lim_{b\to\infty}\!\left[-\dfrac{1}{b}+1\right]=1$.
$\dfrac{1}{x^2}$ decays fast enough for the integral to be finite. The antiderivative $-\dfrac{1}{x}\to 0$ as $x\to\infty$, so the integral converges to 1.

questions.unit_objective: LIM-6.A: Determine convergence of improper integrals with infinite limits (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_1^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $1$ is the value of $\int_1^\infty\dfrac{1}{x^2}\,dx$. For $\dfrac{1}{x}$, the antiderivative $\ln x$ grows without bound as $x\to\infty$.
$\dfrac{1}{x}\to 0$ as $x\to\infty$, but that describes the integrand's limit, not the integral's value. The integral diverges because $\ln x\to\infty$.
$\ln 2 = \int_1^2\dfrac{1}{x}\,dx$, which is only a finite portion of the integral. The full integral $\int_1^\infty\dfrac{1}{x}\,dx$ diverges.
$\int_1^{\infty}\dfrac{1}{x}\,dx=\lim_{b\to\infty}[\ln x]_1^b=\lim_{b\to\infty}(\ln b-0)=\infty$. The integral diverges ($p=1$ is the borderline case).

questions.unit_objective: LIM-6.A: Determine convergence of improper integrals with infinite limits (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{x}{(x+1)(x-2)}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\ln|(x+1)(x-2)|]=\dfrac{2x-1}{(x+1)(x-2)}\neq\dfrac{x}{(x+1)(x-2)}$. Partial fractions decomposition is required.

Partial fractions: $\dfrac{x}{(x+1)(x-2)}=\dfrac{A}{x+1}+\dfrac{B}{x-2}$. At $x=-1$: $-1=-3A\Rightarrow A=\tfrac{1}{3}$. At $x=2$: $2=3B\Rightarrow B=\tfrac{2}{3}$. Integral $=\tfrac{1}{3}\ln|x+1|+\tfrac{2}{3}\ln|x-2|+C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{2}\ln|x^2-x-2|\right]=\dfrac{2x-1}{2(x^2-x-2)}\neq\dfrac{x}{(x+1)(x-2)}$. Use partial fractions decomposition.
The sign of the second term is wrong. At $x=2$: $2=3B$ gives $B=\tfrac{2}{3}$ (positive), so the correct integral has $+\tfrac{2}{3}\ln|x-2|$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.F: Evaluate integrals using partial fraction decomposition (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int x^2 e^x\,dx$?

choices.explanation: The sign of $2e^x$ is wrong. The second integration by parts on $\int xe^x\,dx$ yields $xe^x-e^x$, so distributing the $-2$ gives $-2xe^x+2e^x$ (positive), not $-2e^x$.
Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[x^2e^x]=2xe^x+x^2e^x\neq x^2e^x$. Integration by parts applied twice is required.

Apply integration by parts twice. First: $u=x^2$, $dv=e^x\,dx$: $x^2e^x-2\int xe^x\,dx$. Second (for $\int xe^x\,dx$): $u=x$, $dv=e^x\,dx$: $xe^x-e^x$. Combined: $x^2e^x-2(xe^x-e^x)+C=x^2e^x-2xe^x+2e^x+C$.
This treats $e^x$ as a constant, which is incorrect since $e^x$ depends on $x$. Use integration by parts with $u=x^2$ first.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.E: Evaluate integrals using repeated integration by parts (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^1 \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\pi$ results from the integral over $[-1,1]$: $\arcsin(1)-\arcsin(-1)=\dfrac{\pi}{2}+\dfrac{\pi}{2}=\pi$. From 0 to 1, the result is $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.
The antiderivative is $\arcsin x$, not $x$. Evaluating $\arcsin x$ from 0 to 1 gives $\arcsin(1)-\arcsin(0)=\dfrac{\pi}{2}-0=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.
The integral converges. Although the integrand blows up at $x=1$, $\arcsin x$ remains bounded, and $\lim_{b\to 1^-}\arcsin b=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

This is an improper integral (integrand undefined at $x=1$). $\int\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,dx=\arcsin x+C$. Evaluate: $\lim_{b\to 1^-}[\arcsin x]_0^b=\arcsin 1-\arcsin 0=\dfrac{\pi}{2}-0=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

questions.unit_objective: LIM-6.A: Evaluate improper integrals with discontinuous integrands (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{3x^2+2}{x^3+2x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\ln|3x^2+2|]=\dfrac{6x}{3x^2+2}\neq\dfrac{3x^2+2}{x^3+2x}$. The substitution $u=x^3+2x$ is correct, giving $\ln|x^3+2x|+C$.

The factor $\frac{1}{3}$ is not correct here. The $u$-substitution $u = x^3+2x$ gives $du = (3x^2+2)\,dx$, which matches the numerator exactly — there is no leftover constant to absorb. The antiderivative is $\ln|u|+C = \ln|x^3+2x|+C$, with no leading $\frac{1}{3}$.
Notice the numerator $3x^2+2$ is exactly the derivative of the denominator $x^3+2x$. Let $u=x^3+2x$, $du=(3x^2+2)\,dx$: $\int\dfrac{du}{u}=\ln|u|+C=\ln|x^3+2x|+C$.
This is the reciprocal of the integrand, not its antiderivative. Recognize that the numerator is the derivative of the denominator and apply $u$-substitution.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.D: Evaluate integrals using $u$-substitution by recognizing the $\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)}$ form.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc

stimulus.content: The rate of water flow into a tank, in gallons per minute, is modeled by $r(t) = \dfrac{t}{t^2+1}$ for $t \geq 0$, where $t$ is measured in minutes.

questions.question: Which of the following gives the total amount of water, in gallons, that flows into the tank during the time interval $0 \leq t \leq 2$?

choices.explanation: This is missing the factor of $\dfrac{1}{2}$ from $du=2t\,dt$. The substitution $u=t^2+1$ requires dividing by 2: $\dfrac{1}{2}\int_1^5\dfrac{du}{u}=\dfrac{\ln 5}{2}$.
$\dfrac{\ln 2}{2}$ would result if the upper limit were $t=1$ (giving $u=2$). For $t=2$, $u=t^2+1=5$, so the answer is $\dfrac{\ln 5}{2}$.
$\dfrac{2}{5}$ is the rate $r(2)=\dfrac{2}{4+1}=\dfrac{2}{5}$. This is the instantaneous rate at $t=2$, not the total accumulated flow over $[0,2]$.

questions.unit_objective: CHA-4.D: Interpret and evaluate definite integrals representing accumulation in context.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{2x+3}{x^2+3x-4}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{2}\ln|x^2+3x-4|\right]=\dfrac{2x+3}{2(x^2+3x-4)}\neq\dfrac{2x+3}{x^2+3x-4}$. No factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ appears since $du=(2x+3)\,dx$ exactly matches the numerator.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[\ln|2x+3|]=\dfrac{2}{2x+3}\neq\dfrac{2x+3}{x^2+3x-4}$. Recognize the numerator as the derivative of the denominator and substitute $u=x^2+3x-4$.
Notice the numerator $2x+3$ is exactly the derivative of the denominator $x^2+3x-4$. Let $u=x^2+3x-4$, $du=(2x+3)\,dx$: $\int\dfrac{du}{u}=\ln|u|+C=\ln|x^2+3x-4|+C$.

$\ln|x+4|+\ln|x-1|=\ln|(x+4)(x-1)|=\ln|x^2+3x-4|$. While equivalent, this partial fraction approach is unnecessarily complex when $u$-substitution applies directly: the numerator is the derivative of the denominator.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.D: Evaluate integrals using $u$-substitution by recognizing the $\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)}$ form.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc

stimulus.content: Let $f$ be a twice-differentiable function on $[0, 4]$ such that $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x$ in $[0,4]$.

questions.question: If the trapezoidal sum with 2 equal subintervals is used to approximate $\int_0^4 f(x)\,dx$, which of the following must be true about the trapezoidal approximation $T$?

choices.explanation: The trapezoidal rule is exact only for linear functions ($f''=0$ everywhere). Since $f''>0$, there is a non-zero error and $T\neq \int_0^4 f(x)\,dx$.
Underestimation by the trapezoid rule occurs when $f$ is concave down ($f''<0$). Since $f''>0$ (concave up) here, the trapezoids lie above the curve, so $T$ overestimates.
When $f''(x)>0$ on an interval, $f$ is concave up. The trapezoidal rule overestimates a definite integral when the function is concave up, because the trapezoids lie above the curve.

choices.text: It cannot be determined whether $T$ overestimates or underestimates without knowing $f$
choices.explanation: $f''>0$ throughout $[0,4]$ is sufficient to conclude that $f$ is concave up everywhere on the interval, which guarantees $T$ overestimates the integral.

questions.unit_objective: LIM-5.A: Analyze Riemann sum and trapezoidal approximation errors using concavity.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^{\infty} x e^{-x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $e^{-1}$ is the value of the integrand at $x=1$: $1\cdot e^{-1}$. The integral requires integration by parts and evaluates to 1.
$xe^{-x}\to 0$ as $x\to\infty$, but that is the integrand's limiting value, not the integral's value. The area under $xe^{-x}$ for $x\geq 0$ equals 1.
Exponential decay $e^{-x}$ dominates polynomial growth $x$, so the integral converges. Integration by parts and L'Hopital's Rule confirm it equals 1.

Integration by parts: $u=x$, $dv=e^{-x}dx$; $du=dx$, $v=-e^{-x}$. Result: $\left[-xe^{-x}\right]_0^\infty+\int_0^\infty e^{-x}\,dx$. Boundary: $\lim_{x\to\infty}xe^{-x}=0$ (L'Hopital). Remaining integral: $\left[-e^{-x}\right]_0^\infty=0-(-1)=1$. Total: $0+1=1$.

questions.unit_objective: LIM-6.A: Evaluate improper integrals using integration by parts and limits (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc

stimulus.content: The velocity of a particle moving along the $x$-axis is given by $v(t) = t^2 - 4t + 3$ for $0 \leq t \leq 4$, where $t$ is measured in seconds and $v$ is measured in meters per second.

questions.question: Which of the following gives the total distance traveled by the particle over the interval $0 \leq t \leq 4$?

choices.explanation: $3$ is not the result of a standard accumulation calculation here. The total distance is the sum of $|v(t)|$ over each sign-consistent subinterval: $\tfrac{4}{3}+\tfrac{4}{3}+\tfrac{4}{3}=4$ meters.

$\dfrac{4}{3}$ is the distance on only one of the three subintervals where $v$ has constant sign. Total distance over $[0,4]$ requires summing all three pieces: $\tfrac{4}{3}+\tfrac{4}{3}+\tfrac{4}{3}=4$.
$\dfrac{8}{3}=\int_0^4 v(t)\,dt$ gives net displacement, which accounts for direction. Total distance requires $\int_0^4|v(t)|\,dt=4$ meters.

$v(t)=(t-1)(t-3)$ changes sign at $t=1$ and $t=3$. Total distance $=\int_0^1 v\,dt-\int_1^3 v\,dt+\int_3^4 v\,dt$. Each piece: $\left[\tfrac{t^3}{3}-2t^2+3t\right]_0^1=\tfrac{1}{3}-2+3=\tfrac{4}{3}$; $-\left[\tfrac{t^3}{3}-2t^2+3t\right]_1^3=-(9-18+9-\tfrac{4}{3})=-(-\tfrac{4}{3})=\tfrac{4}{3}$; $\left[\tfrac{t^3}{3}-2t^2+3t\right]_3^4=(\tfrac{64}{3}-32+12)-0=\tfrac{4}{3}$. Total: $\tfrac{4}{3}+\tfrac{4}{3}+\tfrac{4}{3}=4$.

questions.unit_objective: CHA-4.E: Interpret definite integrals to distinguish displacement from total distance.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{e^x}{e^x + 3}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\ln(e^x)+3=x+3$, whose derivative is 1, not $\dfrac{e^x}{e^x+3}$. The correct substitution $u=e^x+3$ gives $\ln(e^x+3)+C$.
With $u=e^x+3$, $\int\dfrac{du}{u}=\ln|u|+C$, not $\dfrac{1}{u^2}+C$. The integral of $\dfrac{1}{u}$ is $\ln|u|$.
Let $u=e^x+3$, $du=e^x\,dx$. Then $\int\dfrac{e^x}{e^x+3}\,dx=\int\dfrac{du}{u}=\ln|u|+C=\ln(e^x+3)+C$ (no absolute value needed since $e^x+3>0$).
Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[e^x\ln(e^x+3)]=e^x\ln(e^x+3)+\dfrac{e^{2x}}{e^x+3}\neq\dfrac{e^x}{e^x+3}$. Use $u$-substitution with $u=e^x+3$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.D: Evaluate integrals using $u$-substitution.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int e^{3x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[3e^{3x}]=9e^{3x}\neq e^{3x}$. The Chain Rule introduces a factor of 3 in the denominator when integrating $e^{3x}$: $\dfrac{1}{3}e^{3x}+C$.
Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[e^{3x}]=3e^{3x}\neq e^{3x}$. The factor of 3 from the Chain Rule requires dividing by 3 when integrating: $\dfrac{1}{3}e^{3x}+C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[e^{3x+1}]=3e^{3x+1}\neq e^{3x}$. Use $u$-substitution with $u=3x$ to get $\dfrac{1}{3}e^{3x}+C$.
Let $u=3x$, $du=3\,dx$: $\dfrac{1}{3}\int e^u\,du=\dfrac{1}{3}e^u+C=\dfrac{1}{3}e^{3x}+C$. Verify: $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{3}e^{3x}\right]=e^{3x}$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-5.A: Determine antiderivatives using $u$-substitution with exponential functions.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: The table shows selected values of a continuous function $f$. Using a right Riemann sum with three equal subintervals, which of the following best approximates $\int_1^7 f(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $48$ does not match any standard Riemann sum from the given table. The right Riemann sum uses $f(3)+f(5)+f(7)=28$ and multiplies by $\Delta x=2$: total $=56$.
$28$ is the sum of the right-endpoint values before multiplying by the width: $f(3)+f(5)+f(7)=10+6+12=28$. Multiply by $\Delta x=2$: $56$.
Three subintervals of width 2: $[1,3]$, $[3,5]$, $[5,7]$. Right endpoints: $f(3)=10$, $f(5)=6$, $f(7)=12$. Right Riemann sum: $2(10+6+12)=2(28)=56$.
$40$ corresponds to the left Riemann sum: $2(f(1)+f(3)+f(5))=2(4+10+6)=2(20)=40$. The right sum uses right endpoints: $f(3),f(5),f(7)$.

questions.unit_objective: LIM-5.A: Approximate definite integrals using right Riemann sums.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin x\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin x\,dx=\left[-\cos x\right]_0^{\pi/2}=-\cos(\pi/2)+\cos(0)=0+1=1$.
$\int_0^\pi\sin x\,dx=0$ (symmetry over a full half-period), but $\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin x\,dx=[-\cos x]_0^{\pi/2}=0-(-1)=1$.
$\int_{\pi/2}^{\pi}\sin x\,dx=-[-\cos x]_{\pi/2}^{\pi}=-(\cos(\pi/2)-\cos\pi)$ is not this calculation. For $[0,\pi/2]$: $[-\cos x]_0^{\pi/2}=-0-(-1)=1$.
$\int_0^\pi\sin x\,dx=[-\cos x]_0^\pi=-\cos\pi+\cos 0=1+1=2$. But the limits here are $0$ to $\pi/2$, giving 1.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.A: Evaluate definite integrals of trigonometric functions using FTC Part 2.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{\ln x}{x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\ln x\cdot\ln x=(\ln x)^2$. While close, the correct antiderivative is $\dfrac{(\ln x)^2}{2}+C$ — a factor of $\dfrac{1}{2}$ is needed from the Power Rule for $\int u\,du$.
Let $u=\ln x$, $du=\dfrac{1}{x}dx$. Then $\int\dfrac{\ln x}{x}\,dx=\int u\,du=\dfrac{u^2}{2}+C=\dfrac{(\ln x)^2}{2}+C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{x^2}\right]=-\dfrac{2}{x^3}\neq\dfrac{\ln x}{x}$. Use $u$-substitution with $u=\ln x$.
Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\dfrac{\ln x}{2x}\right]=\dfrac{\frac{1}{x}\cdot 2x-\ln x\cdot 2}{4x^2}\neq\dfrac{\ln x}{x}$. The $u$-substitution $u=\ln x$ gives $\dfrac{(\ln x)^2}{2}+C$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.D: Evaluate integrals using $u$-substitution.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int x\cos x\,dx$?

choices.explanation: This treats $\cos x$ as a constant, which is incorrect. Integration by parts is required: set $u=x$, $dv=\cos x\,dx$.
This corresponds to $\int x\sin x\,dx$ (integration by parts with $dv=\sin x\,dx$). For $\int x\cos x\,dx$, set $dv=\cos x\,dx$.
Integration by parts: $u=x$, $dv=\cos x\,dx$; $du=dx$, $v=\sin x$. Then $\int x\cos x\,dx=x\sin x-\int\sin x\,dx=x\sin x+\cos x+C$.
The sign of the remaining integral is wrong. $-\int\sin x\,dx=-(-\cos x)+C=+\cos x+C$. So the answer is $x\sin x+\cos x+C$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.E: Evaluate integrals using integration by parts (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{1}{(x-2)(x+3)}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: The sign of the second term is wrong. At $x=-3$: $1=-5B$ gives $B=-\tfrac{1}{5}$ (negative). So the second term is $-\tfrac{1}{5}\ln|x+3|$.
These are antiderivatives of $\dfrac{1}{(x-2)^2}$ and $\dfrac{1}{(x+3)^2}$, not of $\dfrac{1}{(x-2)(x+3)}$. Use partial fractions for linear factors in the denominator.

Partial fractions: $\dfrac{1}{(x-2)(x+3)}=\dfrac{A}{x-2}+\dfrac{B}{x+3}$. At $x=2$: $1=5A\Rightarrow A=\tfrac{1}{5}$. At $x=-3$: $1=-5B\Rightarrow B=-\tfrac{1}{5}$. Integral $=\tfrac{1}{5}\ln|x-2|-\tfrac{1}{5}\ln|x+3|+C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[\ln|(x-2)(x+3)|]=\dfrac{2x+1}{(x-2)(x+3)}\neq\dfrac{1}{(x-2)(x+3)}$. Use partial fractions to decompose before integrating.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.F: Evaluate integrals using partial fraction decomposition (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc

stimulus.content: A particle moves along the $x$-axis with velocity $v(t) = 3t^2 - 6t$ for $t \geq 0$. The particle is at position $x = 5$ when $t = 0$.

questions.question: Which of the following gives the position of the particle at time $t = 3$?

choices.explanation: $\int_0^3 v(t)\,dt=[t^3-3t^2]_0^3=27-27=0$. This is the displacement (change in position), not the final position. Add the initial position: $x(3)=5+0=5$.
$v(3)=3(9)-18=27-18=9$ is the velocity at $t=3$, not the position. Position requires integrating velocity and adding the initial condition: $x(3)=5$.
$32$ might come from $\int_0^3|v(t)|\,dt+5$, but that gives total distance from initial position, not the final position. Net displacement is 0, so $x(3)=5+0=5$.

questions.unit_objective: CHA-4.D: Use accumulation and initial conditions to find position from velocity.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \sec^2(5x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[5\tan(5x)]=25\sec^2(5x)\neq\sec^2(5x)$. The substitution $u=5x$ introduces a factor of $\tfrac{1}{5}$, giving $\tfrac{1}{5}\tan(5x)+C$.
Let $u=5x$, $du=5\,dx$: $\dfrac{1}{5}\int\sec^2 u\,du=\dfrac{1}{5}\tan u+C=\dfrac{1}{5}\tan(5x)+C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[\sec(5x)]=5\sec(5x)\tan(5x)$. The antiderivative of $\sec^2(5x)$ is $\tfrac{1}{5}\tan(5x)+C$, not a secant-tangent expression.
Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\tan(5x)]=5\sec^2(5x)\neq\sec^2(5x)$. The factor from the Chain Rule requires dividing by 5: $\tfrac{1}{5}\tan(5x)+C$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.D: Evaluate integrals of trigonometric functions using $u$-substitution.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc

stimulus.content: The graph of $f$ is shown below (not pictured). It is known that $f$ is positive and increasing on $[1,5]$ and that $\int_1^5 f(x)\,dx = 16$.

questions.question: Which of the following must be true about the left Riemann sum $L$ and the right Riemann sum $R$ with $n$ equal subintervals used to approximate $\int_1^5 f(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: The left sum underestimates when $f$ is increasing, so $L<16$, not $16<L$.

For an increasing function, the left Riemann sum underestimates (left endpoints give smaller values) and the right Riemann sum overestimates (right endpoints give larger values). So $L < \int_1^5 f(x)\,dx < R$.
For an increasing function, right endpoints are larger, so $R>L$. This answer has the inequality reversed.
Both $L$ and $R$ cannot be less than the true integral when $f$ is increasing. The right sum overestimates: $R>16$.

questions.unit_objective: LIM-5.A: Determine over- or underestimation of Riemann sums based on function behavior.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{x+1}{x^2+2x+5}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: This is missing the factor of $\dfrac{1}{2}$. Since $\dfrac{d}{dx}[x^2+2x+5]=2(x+1)$, the substitution introduces $\dfrac{1}{2}$: the correct antiderivative is $\dfrac{1}{2}\ln(x^2+2x+5)+C$.

This form arises when the numerator is a constant and the denominator is an irreducible quadratic: $\int\dfrac{dx}{(x+1)^2+4}=\dfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{x+1}{2}\right)+C$. Here the numerator $x+1$ suggests $u$-substitution instead.

The derivative of $x^2+2x+5$ is $2x+2=2(x+1)$. Let $u=x^2+2x+5$, $du=2(x+1)\,dx$: $\dfrac{1}{2}\int\dfrac{du}{u}=\dfrac{1}{2}\ln|u|+C=\dfrac{1}{2}\ln(x^2+2x+5)+C$ (no abs. value since $x^2+2x+5=(x+1)^2+4>0$).
$\arctan(x+1)+C$ would arise from $\int\dfrac{1}{(x+1)^2+1}\,dx$. This integrand has $(x+1)$ in the numerator and $x^2+2x+5$ in the denominator, requiring $u$-substitution instead.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.D: Evaluate integrals using $u$-substitution by recognizing $\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)}$ patterns.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \sin^2 x\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Use the identity $\sin^2 x=\dfrac{1-\cos 2x}{2}$. Then $\int\sin^2 x\,dx=\dfrac{1}{2}\int(1-\cos 2x)\,dx=\dfrac{x}{2}-\dfrac{\sin 2x}{4}+C$.
Note $-\sin x\cos x=-\dfrac{\sin 2x}{2}$. Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[-\sin x\cos x]=-\cos^2 x+\sin^2 x=-\cos 2x\neq\sin^2 x$. Use the half-angle identity.
The sign of the $\sin 2x$ term is wrong. From $\dfrac{1}{2}\int(1-\cos 2x)\,dx$, integrating $-\cos 2x$ gives $-\dfrac{\sin 2x}{2}$, and dividing by 2 gives $-\dfrac{\sin 2x}{4}$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[-\dfrac{\cos^3 x}{3}\right]=\cos^2 x\sin x\neq\sin^2 x$. The correct approach uses the half-angle identity $\sin^2 x=\dfrac{1-\cos 2x}{2}$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.D: Evaluate integrals using trigonometric identities.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{1}{x^2+4}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\arctan x]=\dfrac{1}{x^2+1}\neq\dfrac{1}{x^2+4}$. Use the formula with $a=2$: $\dfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)+C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{4}\ln(x^2+4)\right]=\dfrac{x}{2(x^2+4)}\neq\dfrac{1}{x^2+4}$. The $\arctan$ form applies here because $x^2+4$ is an irreducible quadratic with constant numerator.

This is missing the factor of $\dfrac{1}{2}$. Using the formula $\int\dfrac{dx}{x^2+a^2}=\dfrac{1}{a}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{x}{a}\right)+C$ with $a=2$ gives $\dfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)+C$.

Write $x^2+4=4\!\left(\dfrac{x^2}{4}+1\right)$. Use the formula $\int\dfrac{dx}{x^2+a^2}=\dfrac{1}{a}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{x}{a}\right)+C$ with $a=2$: $\dfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)+C$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.F: Evaluate integrals involving $\arctan$ using the standard formula (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^4 \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $2\sqrt{x}$ evaluated at $x=4$ is $2(2)=4$. The lower limit contributes $\lim_{a\to 0^+}2\sqrt{a}=0$. So the integral equals $4-0=4$.
Even though the integrand blows up at $x=0$, the integral converges because $p=\tfrac{1}{2}<1$ in $\int_0^a x^{-p}\,dx$, which converges for $p<1$. The value is $4$.
This is an improper integral because the integrand is undefined at $x=0$. $\int_0^4 x^{-1/2}\,dx=\lim_{a\to 0^+}\left[2x^{1/2}\right]_a^4=\lim_{a\to 0^+}(2\cdot 2-2\sqrt{a})=4-0=4$.
$\dfrac{1}{2}$ might come from the exponent in $x^{-1/2}$. The antiderivative of $x^{-1/2}$ is $2x^{1/2}$ (by the Power Rule), evaluated from 0 to 4 gives 4.

questions.unit_objective: LIM-6.A: Evaluate improper integrals with discontinuous integrands at a lower limit (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc

stimulus.content: Let $f$ be a continuous function. It is known that $\int_0^5 f(x)\,dx = 12$ and $\int_0^2 f(x)\,dx = 7$.

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_2^5 f(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $19=12+7$ adds the two integrals instead of subtracting. The additivity property gives $\int_2^5=\int_0^5-\int_0^2=12-7=5$.
Dividing the integrals has no meaning here. By the additivity property: $\int_2^5 f(x)\,dx=\int_0^5 f(x)\,dx-\int_0^2 f(x)\,dx=12-7=5$.
By additivity of definite integrals: $\int_0^5 f(x)\,dx=\int_0^2 f(x)\,dx+\int_2^5 f(x)\,dx$. So $\int_2^5 f(x)\,dx=12-7=5$.
$84=12\times 7$ multiplies the integrals, which has no basis in the additivity property. The answer is $12-7=5$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.A: Apply properties of definite integrals including additivity of intervals.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{1}{x^2-4x+4}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: This simplifies to 0, which cannot be the antiderivative of $\dfrac{1}{(x-2)^2}$. Recognize $(x-2)^2$ as a perfect square and integrate using the Power Rule: $-\dfrac{1}{x-2}+C$.
$\ln|x^2-4x+4|=\ln|(x-2)^2|=2\ln|x-2|$, whose derivative is $\dfrac{2}{x-2}\neq\dfrac{1}{(x-2)^2}$. Factor the denominator as $(x-2)^2$ and use the Power Rule.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{x-2}\right]=-\dfrac{1}{(x-2)^2}\neq\dfrac{1}{(x-2)^2}$. The antiderivative of $(x-2)^{-2}$ is $-(x-2)^{-1}=-\dfrac{1}{x-2}+C$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.F: Evaluate integrals with repeated linear factors in the denominator (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: On which of the following intervals is $f$ decreasing?

choices.explanation: $f'(0)=36>0$ and $f'(1)=(1-4)(1-9)=(-3)(-8)=24>0$. So $f$ is not decreasing on all of $(-3,3)$. It is decreasing only on $(-3,-2)$ and $(2,3)$.
$f'(0)=(0-4)(0-9)=36>0$, so $f$ is increasing near 0. On $(-2,2)$, $f'>0$ throughout. $f$ is decreasing only on $(-3,-2)$ and $(2,3)$.
Test $f'(4)=(16-4)(16-9)=(12)(7)=84>0$. So $f$ is increasing on $(3,5)$. The decreasing intervals are $(-3,-2)$ and $(2,3)$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-5.C: Use FTC Part 1 to determine where an accumulation function is increasing or decreasing.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{5x-3}{(x-1)(x+2)}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: The coefficients are swapped. At $x=1$: $2=3A$ gives $A=\tfrac{2}{3}$ (for the $x-1$ term), and at $x=-2$: $-13=-3B$ gives $B=\tfrac{13}{3}$ (for the $x+2$ term).
The sign of the second coefficient is wrong. At $x=-2$: $-13=-3B$ gives $B=\tfrac{13}{3}$ (positive), so the second term is $+\tfrac{13}{3}\ln|x+2|$.

Partial fractions: $\dfrac{5x-3}{(x-1)(x+2)}=\dfrac{A}{x-1}+\dfrac{B}{x+2}$. At $x=1$: $2=3A\Rightarrow A=\tfrac{2}{3}$. At $x=-2$: $-13=-3B\Rightarrow B=\tfrac{13}{3}$. Integral $=\tfrac{2}{3}\ln|x-1|+\tfrac{13}{3}\ln|x+2|+C$.
These coefficients are incorrect. Partial fractions gives $A=\tfrac{2}{3}$ and $B=\tfrac{13}{3}$, not $A=1$ and $B=2$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.F: Evaluate integrals using partial fraction decomposition (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_2^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{(x-1)^2}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $-\dfrac{1}{x-1}$ evaluated at $x=2$ gives $-1$. But with the limit at $\infty$: $0-(-1)=1$. The integral converges to $+1$, not $-1$.
The integrand $(x-1)^{-2}$ decays like $x^{-2}$ for large $x$, and $\int_2^\infty x^{-p}\,dx$ converges for $p>1$. Here $p=2>1$, so the integral converges to 1.
$(x-1)^{-2}$ decays fast enough to produce a finite integral. The antiderivative $-(x-1)^{-1}\to 0$ as $x\to\infty$, giving a finite limit of 1.
$\int_2^{\infty}(x-1)^{-2}\,dx=\lim_{b\to\infty}\left[-(x-1)^{-1}\right]_2^b=\lim_{b\to\infty}\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{b-1}+1\right)=0+1=1$.

questions.unit_objective: LIM-6.A: Evaluate improper integrals with infinite upper limits (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc

stimulus.content: Let $g(x) = \int_0^x f(t)\,dt$, where the graph of $f$ consists of two line segments: one from $(0,0)$ to $(2,4)$ and one from $(2,4)$ to $(5,-2)$.

questions.question: Which of the following gives $g(5)$?

choices.explanation: $4$ is only the triangular area over $[0,2]$. The signed area over $[2,5]$ equals $4-1=3$. Total: $g(5)=4+3=7$.
$3$ is the signed area over $[2,5]$ only ($4-1=3$). $g(5)$ requires the full integral from 0 to 5: $4+3=7$.
$5=4+1$ might come from treating all areas as positive (total distance rather than signed area). The signed area over $[4,5]$ is $-1$ (below the $x$-axis), giving $g(5)=4+4-1=7$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-5.C: Evaluate accumulation functions using geometric interpretation of area under a curve.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{x^2+1}{x(x^2-1)}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Factor: $x(x^2-1)=x(x-1)(x+1)$. Partial fractions: $\dfrac{x^2+1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}=\dfrac{A}{x}+\dfrac{B}{x-1}+\dfrac{C}{x+1}$. Multiply through by the denominator and evaluate at the roots: at $x=0$, $1=A(0-1)(0+1)=-A$, so $A=-1$. At $x=1$, $2=B(1)(2)=2B$, so $B=1$. At $x=-1$, $2=C(-1)(-2)=2C$, so $C=1$. Integrating each term: $\int\left(-\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x-1}+\frac{1}{x+1}\right)dx = -\ln|x|+\ln|x-1|+\ln|x+1|+C$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[\ln|x(x^2-1)|]=\dfrac{3x^2-1}{x(x^2-1)}\neq\dfrac{x^2+1}{x(x^2-1)}$. Partial fractions decomposition is required.
The coefficient $A$ for the $\tfrac{1}{x}$ term is $-1$, not $+1$. At $x=0$: $1=-A$ gives $A=-1$. The correct integral is $-\ln|x|+\ln|x-1|+\ln|x+1|+C$.

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[-\ln|x|+\tfrac{1}{2}\ln|x^2-1|\right]=-\dfrac{1}{x}+\dfrac{x}{x^2-1}=\dfrac{-x^2+1+x^2}{x(x^2-1)}=\dfrac{1}{x(x^2-1)}\neq\dfrac{x^2+1}{x(x^2-1)}$. Three distinct partial fraction terms are needed.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.F: Evaluate integrals using partial fractions with three distinct linear factors (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{\cos x}{\sin^2 x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Let $u=\sin x$, $du=\cos x\,dx$: $\int\dfrac{du}{u^2}=\int u^{-2}\,du=-u^{-1}+C=-\dfrac{1}{\sin x}+C=-\csc x+C$.
$\cos x\csc x=\dfrac{\cos x}{\sin x}=\cot x$, whose derivative is $-\csc^2 x\neq\dfrac{\cos x}{\sin^2 x}$. Use $u$-substitution with $u=\sin x$.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{\sin x}\right]=-\dfrac{\cos x}{\sin^2 x}$. The antiderivative of $\dfrac{\cos x}{\sin^2 x}$ is $-\dfrac{1}{\sin x}+C$, with a negative sign.
$\dfrac{d}{dx}[-\ln|\sin x|]=-\dfrac{\cos x}{\sin x}=-\cot x$. The integrand is $\dfrac{\cos x}{\sin^2 x}$, not $\dfrac{\cos x}{\sin x}$. Use $u=\sin x$ with Power Rule.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.D: Evaluate integrals of trigonometric expressions using $u$-substitution.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc

stimulus.content: A chemical reaction produces substance $P$ at a rate of $r(t) = \dfrac{4}{t^2+4}$ grams per minute for $0 \leq t \leq 10$, where $t$ is measured in minutes.

questions.question: Which of the following gives the total amount of substance $P$ produced during the first 4 minutes?

choices.explanation: $\ln 5$ would result from a $u$-substitution that applies to rational functions of the form $\dfrac{f'(x)}{f(x)}$, not to $\dfrac{1}{t^2+4}$. The correct approach uses the $\arctan$ formula.

This is missing the factor of $\tfrac{1}{a}=\tfrac{1}{2}$ from the arctan integral formula. $\int\dfrac{4}{t^2+4}\,dt=4\cdot\dfrac{1}{2}\arctan\dfrac{t}{2}=2\arctan\dfrac{t}{2}$. Evaluated: $2\arctan 2$, not $4\arctan 2$.

$\int_0^4\dfrac{4}{t^2+4}\,dt=4\int_0^4\dfrac{dt}{t^2+4}=4\cdot\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\arctan\dfrac{t}{2}\right]_0^4=2\left(\arctan 2-\arctan 0\right)=2\arctan 2$. Wait—$t^2+4$ with $a=2$: $\dfrac{4}{t^2+4}=\dfrac{4}{4(\frac{t^2}{4}+1)}=\dfrac{1}{\frac{t^2}{4}+1}$. So $\int_0^4\dfrac{4}{t^2+4}\,dt=4\cdot\dfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\dfrac{t}{2}\Big|_0^4=2(\arctan 2-0)=2\arctan 2\approx 2(1.107)\approx 2.21$. That is not $\pi$. Let me recalculate: $\int_0^4\dfrac{4}{t^2+4}\,dt$. With $a=2$: formula is $\dfrac{1}{a}\arctan\tfrac{t}{a}$. $\int\dfrac{4}{t^2+4}\,dt=4\cdot\dfrac{1}{2}\arctan\tfrac{t}{2}=2\arctan\tfrac{t}{2}$. At $t=4$: $2\arctan 2$. At $t=0$: $0$. So total $=2\arctan 2\approx 2.214$ grams, not $\pi$.

$\int_0^4\dfrac{4}{t^2+4}\,dt$. Use $\int\dfrac{dx}{x^2+a^2}=\dfrac{1}{a}\arctan\dfrac{x}{a}+C$ with $a=2$. So $\int_0^4\dfrac{4}{t^2+4}\,dt=4\cdot\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\arctan\dfrac{t}{2}\right]_0^4=2\arctan 2-0=2\arctan 2$ grams.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.F: Evaluate integrals using the $\arctan$ formula in context (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \dfrac{x^3}{x^2+1}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Since the degree of the numerator equals the degree of the denominator (both degree-like after long division), perform polynomial long division: $\dfrac{x^3}{x^2+1}=x-\dfrac{x}{x^2+1}$. Then $\int\!\left(x-\dfrac{x}{x^2+1}\right)\!dx=\dfrac{x^2}{2}-\dfrac{1}{2}\ln(x^2+1)+C$.

After long division, $\dfrac{x^3}{x^2+1}=x-\dfrac{x}{x^2+1}$. The second term integrates to $\dfrac{1}{2}\ln(x^2+1)$, not $\arctan x$. $\arctan x+C$ arises from $\int\dfrac{dx}{x^2+1}$, not $\int\dfrac{x\,dx}{x^2+1}$.

The sign of the logarithm term is wrong. Since $\int\dfrac{x}{x^2+1}\,dx=\dfrac{1}{2}\ln(x^2+1)+C$, and we subtract this integral (from the long division), the correct antiderivative is $\dfrac{x^2}{2}-\dfrac{1}{2}\ln(x^2+1)+C$.
Check: $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\dfrac{x^4}{4(x^2+1)}\right]=\dfrac{4x^3(x^2+1)-x^4\cdot 2x}{4(x^2+1)^2}=\dfrac{x^3(x^2+2)}{2(x^2+1)^2}\neq\dfrac{x^3}{x^2+1}$. Use polynomial long division first.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.F: Evaluate integrals of improper rational functions using long division (BC).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc

stimulus.content: A population of bacteria grows at a rate modeled by $P'(t) = \dfrac{100t}{(t^2+1)^2}$ bacteria per hour, where $t$ is measured in hours. The population at time $t=0$ is 500 bacteria.

questions.question: Which of the following gives the population at time $t = 2$?

choices.explanation: $\int_0^2\dfrac{100t}{(t^2+1)^2}\,dt=40$ (computed via $u$-substitution). Adding to the initial population: $500+40=540$, not 520.
$50\ln 5$ would arise from $\int\dfrac{1}{u}\,du$, not $\int u^{-2}\,du$. The substitution $u=t^2+1$ gives $\int u^{-2}\,du=-u^{-1}$, leading to $500+40=540$.

questions.unit_objective: CHA-4.D: Evaluate accumulation integrals to find a quantity's value at a given time.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-bc
questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^{\ln 3} e^x\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $e^{\ln 3}=3$ is the antiderivative evaluated at the upper limit only. Subtracting the lower limit value $e^0=1$ gives $3-1=2$.
$e^{\ln 3}=3$ is only the upper evaluation. Subtract the lower: $3-e^0=3-1=2$.
$\ln 3$ is the upper limit of integration, not the value of the integral. The antiderivative of $e^x$ is $e^x$, so $[e^x]_0^{\ln 3}=3-1=2$.

questions.unit_objective: FUN-6.A: Evaluate definite integrals involving exponential functions using FTC Part 2.
